content.text:
<skill>
<name>ultrawork</name>
<path>/Users/zchee/src/github.com/zchee/agent/codex/skills/ultrawork/SKILL.md</path>
---
name: ultrawork
description: (omx) Parallel execution engine for high-throughput task completion
---

<Purpose>
Ultrawork is a parallel execution engine that runs multiple agents simultaneously for independent tasks. It is a component, not a standalone persistence mode -- it provides parallelism and smart model routing but not persistence, verification loops, or state management.
</Purpose>

<Use_When>
- Multiple independent tasks can run simultaneously
- User says "ulw", "ultrawork", or wants parallel execution
- You need to delegate work to multiple agents at once
- Task benefits from concurrent execution but the user will manage completion themselves
</Use_When>

<Do_Not_Use_When>
- Task requires guaranteed completion with verification -- use `ralph` instead (ralph includes ultrawork)
- Task requires a full autonomous pipeline -- use `autopilot` instead (autopilot includes ralph which includes ultrawork)
- There is only one sequential task with no parallelism opportunity -- delegate directly to an executor agent
- User needs session persistence for resume -- use `ralph` which adds persistence on top of ultrawork
</Do_Not_Use_When>

<Why_This_Exists>
Sequential task execution wastes time when tasks are independent. Ultrawork enables firing multiple agents simultaneously and routing each to the right model tier, reducing total execution time while controlling token costs. It is designed as a composable component that ralph and autopilot layer on top of.
</Why_This_Exists>

<Execution_Policy>
- Fire all independent agent calls simultaneously -- never serialize independent work
- Always pass the `model` parameter explicitly when delegating
- Read `docs/shared/agent-tiers.md` before first delegation for agent selection guidance
- Use `run_in_background: true` for operations over ~30 seconds (installs, builds, tests)
- Run quick commands (git status, file reads, simple checks) in the foreground
</Execution_Policy>

<Steps>
1. **Read agent reference**: Load `docs/shared/agent-tiers.md` for tier selection
2. **Classify tasks by independence**: Identify which tasks can run in parallel vs which have dependencies
3. **Route to correct tiers**:
   - Simple lookups/definitions: LOW tier
   - Standard implementation: STANDARD tier
   - Complex analysis/refactoring: THOROUGH tier
4. **Fire independent tasks simultaneously**: Launch all parallel-safe tasks at once
5. **Run dependent tasks sequentially**: Wait for prerequisites before launching dependent work
6. **Background long operations**: Builds, installs, and test suites use `run_in_background: true`
7. **Verify when all tasks complete** (lightweight):
   - Build/typecheck passes
   - Affected tests pass
   - No new errors introduced
</Steps>

<Tool_Usage>
- Use LOW-tier delegation for simple changes
- Use STANDARD-tier delegation for standard work
- Use THOROUGH-tier delegation for complex work
- Use `run_in_background: true` for package installs, builds, and test suites
- Use foreground execution for quick status checks and file operations
</Tool_Usage>

## State Management

Use `omx_state` MCP tools for ultrawork lifecycle state.

- **On start**:
  `state_write({mode: "ultrawork", active: true, reinforcement_count: 1, started_at: "<now>"})`
- **On each reinforcement/loop step**:
  `state_write({mode: "ultrawork", reinforcement_count: <current>})`
- **On completion**:
  `state_write({mode: "ultrawork", active: false})`
- **On cancellation/cleanup**:
  run `$cancel` (which should call `state_clear(mode="ultrawork")`)

<Examples>
<Good>
Three independent tasks fired simultaneously:
```
delegate(role="executor", tier="LOW", task="Add missing type export for Config interface")
delegate(role="executor", tier="STANDARD", task="Implement the /api/users endpoint with validation")
delegate(role="test-engineer", tier="STANDARD", task="Add integration tests for the auth middleware")
```
Why good: Independent tasks at appropriate tiers, all fired at once.
</Good>

<Good>
Correct use of background execution:
```
delegate(role="executor", tier="STANDARD", task="npm install && npm run build", run_in_background=true)
delegate(role="writer", tier="LOW", task="Update the README with new API endpoints")
```
Why good: Long build runs in background while short task runs in foreground.
</Good>

<Bad>
Sequential execution of independent work:
```
result1 = delegate(executor, LOW, "Add type export")  # wait...
result2 = delegate(executor, STANDARD, "Implement endpoint")     # wait...
result3 = delegate(test-engineer, STANDARD, "Add tests")              # wait...
```
Why bad: These tasks are independent. Running them sequentially wastes time.
</Bad>

<Bad>
Wrong tier selection:
```
delegate(role="executor", tier="THOROUGH", task="Add a missing semicolon")
```
Why bad: THOROUGH tier is expensive overkill for a trivial fix. Use LOW-tier execution instead.
</Bad>
</Examples>

<Escalation_And_Stop_Conditions>
- When ultrawork is invoked directly (not via ralph), apply lightweight verification only -- build passes, tests pass, no new errors
- For full persistence and comprehensive architect verification, recommend switching to `ralph` mode
- If a task fails repeatedly across retries, report the issue rather than retrying indefinitely
- Escalate to the user when tasks have unclear dependencies or conflicting requirements
</Escalation_And_Stop_Conditions>

<Final_Checklist>
- [ ] All parallel tasks completed
- [ ] Build/typecheck passes
- [ ] Affected tests pass
- [ ] No new errors introduced
</Final_Checklist>

<Advanced>
## Relationship to Other Modes

```
ralph (persistence wrapper)
 \-- includes: ultrawork (this skill)
     \-- provides: parallel execution only

autopilot (autonomous execution)
 \-- includes: ralph
     \-- includes: ultrawork (this skill)

ecomode (token efficiency)
 \-- modifies: ultrawork's model selection
```

Ultrawork is the parallelism layer. Ralph adds persistence and verification. Autopilot adds the full lifecycle pipeline. Ecomode adjusts ultrawork's model routing to favor cheaper models.
</Advanced>

</skill>

**Evaluating module imports**

I'm thinking about adding a test to check imports with a local module path. This way, I can ensure that gofumpt groups the standard library, third-party, and local modules correctly. I realize that's important since the ModulePath really does matter for maintaining organization and clarity in my code. Overall, it seems like a good approach to enhance the reliability of these imports. I'll definitely explore this idea further!

**Evaluating template options**

I’m thinking about how the template can produce unused imports, but the generated file will still be valid syntax. I can simply inspect the content during testing. It might be easiest to manage this through template override with just one file and a straightforward package. The generator writes header, helper, and type files, so I could override specific templates with minimal content. One template has an odd import group, but we’ll only inspect the overall output for compilation.

arguments.thought: Regression should directly observe a formatting distinction gofmt would not fix. The strongest case is import grouping: a template override can emit stdlib, third-party, and repo-local imports in the wrong groups; gofumpt with ModulePath should normalize them, while go/format alone would not.

payload.text:
**Evaluating code formatting and testing**

I'm noticing some glitches related to parsing errors from a spawned agent. I need to close those agents after finishing the task. There's a need for implementing gofumpt for code formatting, and I'll inspect the repository further for potential agents and the .github/workflows, though that might not be relevant right now. I should make a minimal change by replacing go/format.Source with gofumpt/format.Source, also managing the module path and go version with go.mod. I'll consider fallback options if errors arise during formatting and possibly add tests, including a regression test using custom templates.

**Clarifying gofumpt testing and formatting**

I need to assert that the test checks for gofumpt formatting, ensuring it fails pre-change and passes post-change. It's important to distinguish between gofmt and gofumpt regarding import grouping with the module path. For example, an import block might include "github.com/zchee/spanner-manager/spannerutil," "cloud.google.com/go/spanner," and "context." While gofmt sorts imports lexically into two groups, it seems like go/format organizes them into stdlib versus non-stdlib groups. Gofmt might merge groups too, which is something to keep in mind!

**Considering codegen options**

I'm thinking about how code generation can function as a library, possibly generating code into other modules. If that's the case, the repo module path won’t correspond to the output module path. Right now, there isn't an option for specifying the module path. I wonder if we should consider adding something like Options.ModulePath for better clarity and functionality. That could really enhance usability!

**Assessing Go Options Configuration**

I’m considering that the Options struct needs LangVersion and ModulePath. The format.Source function requires this Options parameter, but fields can be left empty. LangVersion defaults to "go1" if not specified, so maybe I should set it based on `go 1.26`? The documentation implies the empty default is fine, but given that the repo uses generics, I should probably align it with the module's go directive by parsing the nearest `go.mod` file. That seems like a solid plan!

**Exploring Generator Path Resolution**

I’m wondering if NewGenerator is aware of the location of the generator.go file. It might utilize `debug.ReadBuildInfo()` for that. The Main.Path refers to the module path, while Main.Version doesn’t indicate the Go version but might be found through GoVersion. I need to verify if BuildInfo includes Main.Path and GoVersion fields. If I compile a package for tests or binaries, will readbuildinfo return the correct main module path? For `go test` in the repo, Main.Path could possibly be `github.com/zchee/spanner-manager/codegen.test`.

**Evaluating Go module detection**

I need to address the t.TempDir() test cases, and it looks like I don't have a go.mod from outDir, but that shouldn't be an issue. Instead, my focus should be on detecting the nearest go.mod file upwards from OutDir and then parsing the module and Go version from it. This approach seems robust; when I'm generating within a module tree, it makes sense to use that module's go.mod. If not, I simply won’t have a module path.

**Analyzing Go dependencies and code generation**

Alright, I’m looking at the requirements for version v0.9.2, which needs Go 1.24, but the repo uses 1.26. Since it’s in vendor mode, I’ll consider adding a direct dependency. I think it’s best to use the module path from the current module and the version from go.mod for formatting the generated code. I should avoid hardcoding values, so maybe parsing from go.mod is the way to go. I’ll need to figure out how the generator library works in terms of the repo root.

**Considering module path for code generation**

I realized that setting the ModulePath always to spanner-manager's module path wouldn't work for external users. I need to think this through more carefully. I should check the generator options, as it might not currently include a module path. Maybe we can add an option? The user wants to implement formatting for generated code with the gofumpt package, so we need to ensure that for import grouping, the module path matches the target module, not the generator's path.

**Evaluating workflow tasks**

I need to inspect the .github workflow, as it might not be tracked or could be irrelevant. After delegating tasks, I want to update the plan and check the progress on implementation. It could be a good idea to close subagents after integration. I'm considering direct file reads, particularly from codegen/generator.go and its tests. Also, I might inspect `go env GOMOD` or utilize `go list -m` to get the nearest module, but using external Go commands may not be ideal for sandboxing. Pure Go might be a better choice.

**Evaluating test execution**

I need to run local tests to gather evidence. Maybe I can use a subagent for the main tests and try a quick local compile. The rule states we should always use the test-runner agent for executing tests, so I might delegate that. Although, I might still want to run non-test commands myself, like gofmt. The verification loop likely needs direct observation of outputs. It seems I should run `go test` locally, so let’s follow the instruction with the subagent. There were some faulty responses before, possibly due to a tool issue.

payload.text:
**Organizing test dependencies**

I noticed that some test-only dependencies were added to go.sum, and it seems x/mod is gone from go.mod — which is alright since it’s not required. I should check the vendor diff for any new entries and be sure the vendor includes the necessary packages. The instructions say to use a test-runner agent for executing tests. I’m considering having a separate verifier for repo-wide tests and running everything in parallel. It might be good to update step statuses before testing and also run `git diff --check` and `go vet` locally.

**Planning test execution**

I’m thinking it might be effective to use one test engineer for `go test ./codegen` and another verifier for the full command `go test ./... && go vet ./... && git diff --check`, even if there aren't any tests. Running the full repo tests in parallel should be achievable, but I need to close previous agents now to keep things organized. It’s better to avoid clutter, so I’ll close agents like `/root/codegen_surface_scan` and `/root/gofumpt_test_plan` and update the plan accordingly.

status.completed:
Read-only analysis complete. No files edited.

## Best regression test to add

**Most direct test:** assert the **gofumpt-only header rewrite** in the generated header file.

Why this is the best one:
- The formatter switch happens centrally in `codegen/generator.go:329-337`.
- The generated header already exists in every run and is easy to inspect with existing helpers.
- I confirmed an actual `gofumpt` diff on generated output: `gofumpt` rewrites the header’s four standalone `_ = ...` lines into a single grouped `var (...)` block, while current `go/format` output does not.

### Exact failing-before / passing-after snippet

Target file:
- generated `spanner_header.spanner.go` or `spanner_header.yo.go`

Exact snippet to assert:
```go
// Ensure imports are used.
var (
	_ = context.Background
	_ = fmt.Sprintf
	_ = spanner.Key{}
	_ = iterator.Done
)
```

### Best place to add it
**Option A, smallest diff:** extend the existing header subtest in  
`codegen/generator_test.go:53-61`

That test already reads:
- `spanner_header.yo.go`
- with `readTextFile(...)`

So the minimal regression is just one more `contains` entry for the grouped `var (` block.

### Why this is stronger than a generic “run gofumpt” check
It is:
- deterministic,
- localized,
- directly tied to a real generated artifact,
- guaranteed to fail under current `go/format` behavior,
- guaranteed to pass after the formatter switch.

---

## Second-best regression test

If you want a test closer to “real model output” rather than header scaffolding, use the existing custom-import fixture in:

- `codegen/generator_test.go:232-275` `TestGenerator_Generate_CustomTypeImports`

`gofumpt` changes the import block in `users.spanner.go` from the current awkward grouping to this normalized layout:

```go
import (
	"context"
	"fmt"

	"cloud.google.com/go/spanner"

	json "encoding/json"
)
```

That is also a **real observed diff** from this repo’s generator output.

### Tradeoff vs header test
- **Header test:** simplest, universal, lowest maintenance.
- **Custom-import test:** more representative of “actual generated model file” formatting, but slightly more fixture-coupled.

---

## Evidence anchors

### Formatter switch point
- `codegen/generator.go:329-337`
  - current code calls `format.Source(buf.Bytes())`

### Existing useful test surfaces
- `codegen/generator_test.go:29-87` `TestGenerator_Generate_BasicFiles`
- `codegen/generator_test.go:232-275` `TestGenerator_Generate_CustomTypeImports`
- `codegen/generator_test.go:335-422` `TestGenerator_Generate_CompileGeneratedOutput`

### Actual `gofumpt` diffs I verified
Observed with generated files from repo fixtures and `/Users/zchee/go/bin/gofumpt -d`:
1. `spanner_header.spanner.go`
   - four standalone `var _ = ...` lines become one `var (...)` block
2. `users.spanner.go` and `runs.spanner.go`
   - import ordering/grouping changes to gofumpt style

---

## Recommended test shape

### Recommendation 1: minimum diff
Add one exact substring assertion to the existing header test:
- file: `codegen/generator_test.go`
- test: `TestGenerator_Generate_BasicFiles/header`

Assert the generated header contains the grouped `var (...)` block above.

### Recommendation 2: slightly stronger
Add a new targeted test, e.g.:
- `TestGenerator_Generate_GofumptHeaderFormatting`

Generate a basic schema, read `spanner_header.spanner.go`, and assert the exact grouped block.

### Recommendation 3: strongest long-term
Add a helper that compares selected generated files to `gofumpt` output and asserts they are already identical.

Tradeoff:
- strongest semantic guarantee,
- but introduces tool-coupling in tests and depends on `gofumpt` availability in the test environment.

Given this repo’s current test style, **I would not start there**.

---

## Broader verification commands

For the formatting change itself, I’d verify in this order:

1. **Focused package tests**
   ```bash
   go test ./codegen
   ```

2. **Full repo tests**
   ```bash
   go test ./...
   ```

3. **Match CI’s stronger lane**
   From `.github/workflows/test.yaml:34-36`
   ```bash
   go test -v -race -count=1 -shuffle=on -cover -covermode=atomic -coverpkg=./... -coverprofile=coverage.out -tags='osusergo,netgo,static' -ldflags='-s -w' ./...
   ```

4. **Vet**
   From `.github/workflows/test.yaml:61-63`
   ```bash
   go vet ./...
   ```

If you want only the narrowest smoke test for the formatter migration, the most relevant command is still:

```bash
go test ./codegen
```

---

## Bottom line

**Recommended exact regression:** extend `TestGenerator_Generate_BasicFiles` to assert the generated header contains the grouped:

```go
var (
	_ = context.Background
	_ = fmt.Sprintf
	_ = spanner.Key{}
	_ = iterator.Done
)
```

That is the cleanest test I found that:
- **fails before** replacing `go/format` with `gofumpt`,
- **passes after** the switch,
- fits the current test style with minimal churn.

Confidence:
- Cost-effectiveness: **0.98**
- Scalability: **0.95**
- Reliability: **0.97**

I attempted to inspect `codegen/generator.go` and the surrounding repository (per your read-only scan request) but every tool call failed immediately with `exec` parsing errors (“SyntaxError: Unexpected token ':'”) because the agent runtime insists on receiving raw script text instead of the usual JSON-wrapped payload. Without being able to run `shell_command`, `read_text_file`, or any code-introspection tool, I can’t actually open `codegen/generator.go`, the related tests, or the docs/CLI files you referenced.

Once the tool blockage is resolved (or if you can paste the relevant files), here’s the concrete, file-by-file path I would follow:

1. **Locate the formatting hook in `codegen/generator.go`.** Look for the call that currently marshals generated Go into a buffer and runs `go/format` on it (likely via `format.Source` or `format.Node`). The symbol(s) to touch are the function that takes the generated AST/text and writes it to disk—probably around `generateFile`, `emitFile`, or `writeFormatted`. That’s where you’d swap `go/format` for `mvdan.cc/gofumpt`, so the formatting path for every generated file goes through the gofumpt API instead of the standard formatter.

2. **Update dependency and invocation.** Add `mvdan.cc/gofumpt` to `go.mod` (with a specific semantic version that matches your Go version) and replace `format.Source` calls with `gofumpt.Source`. If there’s any CLI flag or config option controlling `gofmt`/formatting, document the new dependency and default, and consider adding a flag (under `docs/` or CLI reference) to opt out or switch formatting back to `gofmt` for rapid iteration.

3. **Adjust tests/docs.** Identify any tests under `codegen/...` or `tests/...` that assert exact formatting output. They’ll need to be updated to reflect gofumpt’s stricter/sharper formatting (e.g., `gofumpt` adds parentheses around composite literals and enforces consistent spacing). Also, update any CLI documentation or README sections describing `gofmt` runs to mention `gofumpt`, including any environment variables (module path/version, Go language version) that gofumpt respects differently.

4. **Pitfalls to flag.**
   - **Module path/version**: Ensure `go.mod` still allows `mvdan.cc/gofumpt` (which depends on Go 1.18+). If your GOPATH or module proxy doesn’t include it, you’ll need to vendor or mirror it.
   - **Language version mismatch**: Gofumpt uses go/token/ast matching the Go 1.20+ spec; make sure `go` directives in `go.mod` align so generated code’s gofmt/gofumpt output stays consistent with your target.
   - **CLI flag docs**: If the generator exposes `--fmt` or similar, document how to choose between gofmt and gofumpt, and note that a dependency change may change the generated output.

Please let me know if you can provide the relevant source files (or fix tool access) so I can double-check the exact symbols and produce a precise set of edits and file paths before editing anything.

- guaranteed to pass after the formatter switch.

---

## Second-best regression test

If you want a test closer to “real model output” rather than header scaffolding, use the existing custom-import fixture in:

- `codegen/generator_test.go:232-275` `TestGenerator_Generate_CustomTypeImports`

`gofumpt` changes the import block in `users.spanner.go` from the current awkward grouping to this normalized layout:

```go
import (
	"context"
	"fmt"

	"cloud.google.com/go/spanner"

	json "encoding/json"
)
```

That is also a **real observed diff** from this repo’s generator output.

### Tradeoff vs header test
- **Header test:** simplest, universal, lowest maintenance.
- **Custom-import test:** more representative of “actual generated model file” formatting, but slightly more fixture-coupled.

---

## Evidence anchors

### Formatter switch point
- `codegen/generator.go:329-337`
  - current code calls `format.Source(buf.Bytes())`

### Existing useful test surfaces
- `codegen/generator_test.go:29-87` `TestGenerator_Generate_BasicFiles`
- `codegen/generator_test.go:232-275` `TestGenerator_Generate_CustomTypeImports`
- `codegen/generator_test.go:335-422` `TestGenerator_Generate_CompileGeneratedOutput`

### Actual `gofumpt` diffs I verified
Observed with generated files from repo fixtures and `/Users/zchee/go/bin/gofumpt -d`:
1. `spanner_header.spanner.go`
   - four standalone `var _ = ...` lines become one `var (...)` block
2. `users.spanner.go` and `runs.spanner.go`
   - import ordering/grouping changes to gofumpt style

---

## Recommended test shape

### Recommendation 1: minimum diff
Add one exact substring assertion to the existing header test:
- file: `codegen/generator_test.go`
- test: `TestGenerator_Generate_BasicFiles/header`

Assert the generated header contains the grouped `var (...)` block above.

### Recommendation 2: slightly stronger
Add a new targeted test, e.g.:
- `TestGenerator_Generate_GofumptHeaderFormatting`

Generate a basic schema, read `spanner_header.spanner.go`, and assert the exact grouped block.

### Recommendation 3: strongest long-term
Add a helper that compares selected generated files to `gofumpt` output and asserts they are already identical.

Tradeoff:
- strongest semantic guarantee,
- but introduces tool-coupling in tests and depends on `gofumpt` availability in the test environment.

Given this repo’s current test style, **I would not start there**.

---

## Broader verification commands

For the formatting change itself, I’d verify in this order:

1. **Focused package tests**
   ```bash
   go test ./codegen
   ```

2. **Full repo tests**
   ```bash
   go test ./...
   ```

3. **Match CI’s stronger lane**
   From `.github/workflows/test.yaml:34-36`
   ```bash
   go test -v -race -count=1 -shuffle=on -cover -covermode=atomic -coverpkg=./... -coverprofile=coverage.out -tags='osusergo,netgo,static' -ldflags='-s -w' ./...
   ```

4. **Vet**
   From `.github/workflows/test.yaml:61-63`
   ```bash
   go vet ./...
   ```

If you want only the narrowest smoke test for the formatter migration, the most relevant command is still:

```bash
go test ./codegen
```

---

## Bottom line

**Recommended exact regression:** extend `TestGenerator_Generate_BasicFiles` to assert the generated header contains the grouped:

```go
var (
	_ = context.Background
	_ = fmt.Sprintf
	_ = spanner.Key{}
	_ = iterator.Done
)
```

That is the cleanest test I found that:
- **fails before** replacing `go/format` with `gofumpt`,
- **passes after** the switch,
- fits the current test style with minimal churn.

Confidence:
- Cost-effectiveness: **0.98**
- Scalability: **0.95**
- Reliability: **0.97**"
    }
  },
  "plan": {}
}

**Addressing tracked files and vendor directory**

I need to mention only the changed tracked files. I could point out that the vendor directory was updated in the working tree but is ignored and not tracked. The vendor tree, generated by Go mod, is also ignored, which means if others don't run vendor, they won't receive updates from patches. My changed code imports mvdan.cc/gofumpt, and tests passed locally due to the vendor tree in the working directory. However, if the CI environment expects the vendor directory, it might not function properly since it’s gitignored and not committed.

**Considering vendor directory and dependencies**

If a clone doesn't include the vendor directory, it builds fine with internet access. I need to mention that while the vendor directory is ignored and not tracked, it's not an issue unless offline. In this working tree, the vendor dir exists for Go 1.26. If someone checks out the repo without the vendor, module mode still works fine. However, if the CI environment relies on the vendor, it won't function. We ran tests locally using the vendor, but if it's missing in other environments, it'll fetch modules since the go.mod has the dependency. But if the policy expects a tracked vendor, that won't be the case.

**Addressing code documentation**

I'm seeing a potential issue here: we haven't modified the generated code templates or CLI documentation. The request only asked for formatting changes, so that’s okay. I need to make sure to mention that if no new errors were introduced and there's no pending work, I can consider the task complete. I'll note that all requested verifications have passed. Let's keep everything clear and straightforward!

payload.message:
Mode: ultrawork  
Result: completed — generated Go code now formats with `gofumpt`

What changed

- `codegen/generator.go`
  - Replaced the inline `go/format.Source(...)` path with a dedicated `formatGoSource(...)` helper.
  - Switched formatting to `mvdan.cc/gofumpt/format.Source(...)`.
  - Added `detectGoModuleConfig(...)` and `parseGoModMetadata(...)` so generated code formatting uses:
    - `ModulePath` from the nearest `go.mod`
    - `LangVersion` from the nearest `go.mod`
  - Kept the prior fallback behavior: if formatting fails, the generator still writes the unformatted source for debugging rather than hard-failing.

- `codegen/generator_test.go`
  - Strengthened the existing basic generator test to assert a real gofumpt-visible change in the generated header:
    - the four `_ = ...` lines are now grouped into a single `var (...)` block
  - Added `TestDetectGoModuleConfig` to verify nearest-`go.mod` discovery and the empty-config fallback.

- `go.mod`
  - Added direct dependency: `mvdan.cc/gofumpt v0.9.2`

- `go.sum`
  - Added checksums required by the new dependency resolution.

Implementation choice and tradeoffs

I chose the in-process gofumpt library API over the two obvious alternatives:

1. Use `gofumpt` as a Go library  
   - Chosen
   - Pros: deterministic, no subprocess overhead, works inside existing generator flow, testable
   - Cons: adds a module dependency

2. Shell out to a `gofumpt` binary
   - Rejected
   - Pros: simple conceptually
   - Cons: brittle runtime dependency, harder to test, depends on external tool availability

3. Hardcode module path / Go version
   - Rejected
   - Pros: smaller code diff
   - Cons: wrong import grouping and language-version behavior outside this repo layout

Simplifications made

- Centralized all generated Go formatting in one helper instead of leaving formatter logic inline in `writeTemplate`.
- Auto-detected module metadata from `go.mod` instead of adding new CLI flags or threading formatter config through more call sites.
- Reused the existing basic generation test for the main regression instead of introducing a heavier golden-file harness.

Exact verification run

Passed:
- `git diff --check`
- `go test ./codegen`
- `go test ./...`
- `go vet ./...`

Observed outputs:
- `go test ./codegen`  
  - `ok github.com/zchee/spanner-manager/codegen 1.297s`
- `go test ./...`
  - `?    github.com/zchee/spanner-manager [no test files]`
  - `ok   github.com/zchee/spanner-manager/cmd 0.023s`
  - `ok   github.com/zchee/spanner-manager/codegen (cached)`
  - `ok   github.com/zchee/spanner-manager/diff 0.010s`
  - `ok   github.com/zchee/spanner-manager/migration 0.028s`
  - `ok   github.com/zchee/spanner-manager/spannerutil 0.023s`
  - `ok   github.com/zchee/spanner-manager/sqlutil 0.009s`

Current tracked file status

Modified tracked files:
- `codegen/generator.go`
- `codegen/generator_test.go`
- `go.mod`
- `go.sum`

Untouched pre-existing untracked path:
- `.github/workflows/`

Remaining risks

- `vendor/mvdan.cc/gofumpt/...` exists in the local working tree after vendoring, but `vendor/` is gitignored here, so it is not tracked. A clean clone will rely on normal module download rather than checked-in vendored code.
- Formatting still intentionally falls back to writing unformatted output if gofumpt returns an error. That preserves existing debugging behavior, but it means malformed generated templates still won’t hard-fail on formatter error.

Confidence

- Cost-effectiveness: 0.96
- Scalability: 0.94
- Reliability: 0.95

No pending work remains for the requested change.
